title: Merck — Oncology — Synthesis
html:
Merck Oncology Cross-Study Synthesis

Generated: 2026-04-26 (enriched with HNSCC quantitative data) Studies analyzed: 9 Files parsed: KN-689 HNSCC Final Report v4 (Pre-read), HNSCC LA Baseline ATU W1 (n=134), Keytruda NSCLC HCP-Pt Study vF, LA HNSCC Biomarker Testing Qual v7.0, HNSCC LA Baseline ATU W1, SubQ IO Kickoff, KN-689 Listening Sessions (multiple waves), KN-689 HNSCC Final Report v3, Topline Presentation v2 Findings extracted: Manual extraction from ~400K+ chars of parsed PPTX content across 9 study deliverables Method: Cross-study pattern matching against Vinoth's Merck oncology thesis tracker

1. Overview

This synthesis integrates findings from nine ZoomRx studies conducted for Merck's Keytruda oncology franchise between October 2024 and April 2026. The studies span two tumor types (NSCLC and HNSCC), four methodologies (HCP-Patient conversations, qualitative TDIs, quantitative ATU survey, listening sessions), and cover the full KN-689 launch arc from pre-launch biomarker research through post-launch experience tracking. The SubQ IO kickoff adds a cross-tumor formulation dimension.

The central question across all studies: What stands between Keytruda's clinical data and its commercial adoption in earlier-stage, perioperative settings?

2. Cross-Study Patterns

Pattern 1: The Referral Chain Is the Universal Bottleneck — Across Both NSCLC and HNSCC

This is the most consistent finding across every study in the portfolio. The referring physician (non-oncologist) controls the gate to IO consideration.

NSCLC HCP-Pt Study (vF): Only 59% of Stage 2+ patients were referred to an oncologist to discuss neoadjuvant treatment. Pulmonologists and surgeons frequently defer IO discussion to oncologists, believing it is "not their role." 27% of conversations mention urgency to treat surgically. 53% of conversations do not mention MDTs.

HNSCC KN-689 Final Report (v4, n=7 conversations + n=14 interviews): In 7 HCP-Patient conversations, KN-689 was discussed in 5 but only considered for use in 3. One community surgeon was "not yet convinced by KN689 data" (C5). One patient preferred immediate surgery over NAT to avoid a feeding tube (C2). The referral from surgeon to Med Onc for NAT IO assessment is the critical handoff — and H&N surgeons "own patient case discussion since they see the patient before Oncs."

HNSCC ATU W1 Baseline (n=134, March 2025): Surgeons present a significantly higher proportion of patients in formal MDT/tumor boards than oncologists perceive: 85% vs. 65% (SURGs vs ONCs respectively). SURGs personally present ~13 LA HNSCC patients to MDTs per quarter; ONCs present ~6. This 20-point perception gap suggests surgeons and oncologists have different mental models of what "discussing" a patient means.

HNSCC Biomarker Testing Qual (v7.0): Surgeons are primarily bringing LA HNSCC patients to MDT, but 20-40% of Stage III-IVA patients are not discussed pre-surgery. Surgeons who prefer direct resection can bypass the MDT entirely. The biomarker testing qual found a clear hierarchy: academic collaborators bring >90% of patients; community traditionalists bring <50%.

What this means together: Whether in NSCLC (Pulm/Surg → Onc) or HNSCC (Surg → MDT → Onc), the referring physician (non-oncologist) controls the gate. If they don't refer, the oncologist never gets to discuss IO. And the reasons for not referring are consistent across tumor types: urgency to treat, belief that surgery is curative, lack of comfort discussing IO, and institutional workflow gaps.

Implication for Merck: The field force strategy cannot be oncologist-focused alone. Surgeon and pulmonologist engagement is essential — but the message cannot be about Keytruda specifically. It must be about the full treatment pathway and the importance of oncology referral before surgery.

Pattern 2: PD-L1 Testing Maturity Predicts KN-689 Adoption — And the Data Now Quantifies the Gap

Biomarker Testing Qual (v7.0): Only 3 of 35 US HCPs had reflexive PD-L1 testing protocols in place for LA HNSCC at time of study (Dec 2024-Jan 2025). ~50% of respondents expected reflexive protocols to be implemented upon KN-689 availability, but implementation timelines range from "few weeks" to ">1 year."

KN-689 Final Report (v4, Feb 2026): By post-launch, testing maturity has improved but remains uneven:

  • 4/13 practices have established reflexive PD-L1 testing protocols (typically academic settings)
  • 6/13 are "moving toward" reflexive testing (protocol acknowledged but not formalized)
  • 3/13 remain manual, per-case ordering (typically community settings)
  • 1 HCP reported not conducting PD-L1 testing at all ("don't prescribe NAT IO in their practice")

Process barriers are more common than prohibitive barriers:

  • Delayed triggers (n=6): tests ordered only upon receipt of negative p16 results, or deferred until MDT meeting
  • Lack of HCP accountability (n=5): H&N surgeons aware of KN-689 but rely on Med Oncs to order tests
  • Poor tissue samples (n=3): FNA biopsies may require additional biopsy for PD-L1
  • Insurance coverage issues (n=3): partial/no coverage for Medicare/Medicaid patients

ATU W1 Baseline (n=134): 82% of ONCs and 70% of SURGs personally order biomarker tests — but ordering rates differ from reflexive protocol implementation. Community ONCs: 72% self-reported KN-689 knowledge vs. Academic ONCs: 62% — community oncologists claim higher knowledge, possibly reflecting guideline adherence over nuanced understanding.

What this means together: PD-L1 testing is the necessary prerequisite for KN-689 prescription, and the testing infrastructure is maturing but not mature. The gap between "I know KN-689 exists" (70% aided knowledge) and "my practice can identify eligible patients reflexively" (only 4/13 practices) is the adoption bottleneck. Four specific leverage points: (1) minimize FNA biopsy, (2) encourage reflexive protocols, (3) prompt surgeons to order PD-L1 at biopsy, (4) ensure all Stage III-IVA patients reach MDT.

Implication for ZoomRx: A longitudinal biomarker testing tracker — measuring reflexive adoption rates, FNA usage, and PD-L1 TAT by practice type — would be highly valuable to Merck's launch team. This is a trackable, measurable leading indicator of KN-689 adoption.

Pattern 3: Surgeons and Oncologists Operate in Parallel Universes — The MDT Is the Only Bridge

NSCLC HCP-Pt Study: Surgeons and pulmonologists speak at a 5th-grade reading level with patients, spending 85% of the conversation talking. Patients focus on surgery logistics, recovery, and quitting smoking — not systemic treatment. Surgeons are more likely to discuss neoadjuvant than pulmonologists, but even among surgeons, the discussion is brief.

HNSCC KN-689 Final Report (v4): The role division is clearly defined:

  • H&N Surgeons: "Own patient case discussion since they see the patient before Oncs." Assess resectability and feasibility of surgery. "May push for direct surgery in case of extremely easily accessible tumor."
  • Med Oncs: "Assess eligibility and appropriateness of neoadjuvant treatments, including IO." Order PD-L1 test for NAT IO eligibility.
  • In "extremely few cases," H&N surgeons may schedule surgery before the MDT if the tumor is easily accessible — bypassing the collaborative discussion entirely.

ATU W1 (n=134): The surgeon-oncologist perception gap is quantified:

  • SURGs present 85% of LA HNSCC patients in formal tumor boards; ONCs report 65%
  • SURGs personally present ~13 patients to MDTs quarterly; ONCs present ~6
  • 36% of ONCs discuss patients in informal physician networks vs. only 15% of SURGs — oncologists compensate for limited formal MDT access with informal channels
  • Both SURGs (72%) and ONCs (55%) strive for mutual consensus when NAT is determined to be needed — collaborative intent is high, but the structural gap in presentation rates suggests different thresholds for "warranting MDT discussion"

Biomarker Testing Qual: The surgeon-oncologist divide on PD-L1 testing is stark. Surgeons view testing as "the oncologist's role." Yet the ideal workflow — PD-L1 results available at the first MDT — requires the surgeon to order testing at diagnosis. This is a coordination problem, not a knowledge problem.

What this means together: The MDT meeting is not just a decision point — it's the only place where the surgeon's surgical assessment, the oncologist's IO expertise, and the pathologist's biomarker results converge. When the MDT works well, KN-689 gets discussed. When it doesn't (20-40% of patients), the default is surgery without IO.

Implication for Merck: MDT-level interventions (decision support tools, checklist protocols, PD-L1 ordering workflows) may be more effective than individual HCP messaging. Merck should consider supporting institutional pathway development rather than relying solely on field force detailing.

Pattern 4: KN-689 Drivers Are Both Immediate and Future-Oriented — HCPs Are Confident About Managing Barriers

KN-689 Final Report (v4): HCP interviews (n=14) reveal the strongest drivers of KN-689 consideration:

  • Short-term goal — lower tumor burden before surgery (n=11/14, 79%)
  • Long-term goal — lower risk of recurrence (n=11/14, 79%)
  • FDA approval, NCCN guideline inclusion, better tolerability (n=4/14, 29%)

Key barriers:

  • Insurance/logistical issues (access to infusion centers) — can potentially prohibit prescription (n=6/14, 43%)
  • Concern with tumor progression — HCPs intend to address through regular monitoring (n=5/14, 36%)
  • Patient hesitation due to personal goals and long treatment duration (n=3/14, 21%)

Critically: HCPs indicate that most barriers "would not impede future KN689 consideration." The barriers are manageable, not prohibitive. Positive experiences include better surgical resection margins, reduced surgical burden, and favorable tolerability.

HCP-Patient Conversations (n=7): KN-689 is presented to patients as:

  1. An immunotherapy (highlight pathway mechanism)
  2. Helps shrink tumor / reduce scope of resection
  3. Has potential side effects, but better tolerated than chemo
  4. Improves long-term survival benefits

What this means: The product perception story is strongly positive. The adoption bottleneck is infrastructure (testing, MDT, access) not physician conviction. This is a favorable dynamic — infrastructure gaps are fixable with investment; conviction gaps require clinical data.

Pattern 5: Community vs. Academic Is the Defining Adoption Axis — With a Counterintuitive Twist

Biomarker Testing Qual: Academic institutions with in-house PD-L1 testing capabilities are 3-4x more likely to have reflexive protocols. Community practices lack in-house capabilities, strong advocates, and the financial analysis to justify universal testing.

ATU W1 (n=134) — The surprise finding: Community ONCs are more likely to prescribe the complete KN-689 regimen: 32% community vs. 15% academic. Similarly, community SURGs report higher KN-689 knowledge (80%) than academic SURGs (64%). This counterintuitive pattern may reflect:

  • Community oncologists' greater willingness to follow guidelines once they adopt (vs. academic tendency to individualize)
  • Academic caution about novel perioperative regimens (preference for clinical trial enrollment over commercial use)
  • Self-reported knowledge bias (community HCPs may overstate familiarity)

NSCLC HCP-Pt Study: Community MDs are more likely to lack MDT access and more likely to defer treatment decisions to other specialties. Some community settings don't have tumor boards at all.

KN-689 Final Report (v4): The 14 HCP qualitative interviews skewed academic (typical for research panels), but the 7 HCP-Patient conversations included community surgeons — where the most revealing barriers surfaced (C5: community surgeon "not yet convinced by KN689 data").

What this means together: The academic-community divide is the single most important segmentation variable for predicting KN-689 adoption, but the direction of the effect is nuanced. Academic centers lead on infrastructure (reflexive testing, active MDTs) but may lag on prescribing intent. Community practices lead on prescribing willingness but lag on infrastructure. The implication: different interventions are needed for each segment.

Pattern 6: Unaided Awareness Is Surgeon-Led — But Nearly Half of All HCPs Recall Nothing

ATU W1 (n=134, pre-launch March 2025):

  • Unaided KEYTRUDA awareness for LA HNSCC: SURGs 47% vs ONCs 29% — significantly higher among surgeons
  • ~50% of both SURGs and ONCs do not recall ANY new products or products in development for LA HNSCC
  • Opdivo regimen awareness (aided): ONCs 63%, SURGs 61% — the competitive comparator is well-known
  • Unmet needs (unaided): improved patient outcomes, overall survival, better treatment options, low side effects

What this means: At pre-launch baseline, there's a massive awareness gap to close. Half of the target HCP universe is not thinking about new treatments for LA HNSCC at all. The fact that surgeons lead on unaided awareness is encouraging (they're the gatekeeper specialty) but the absolute level (47%) means more than half of surgeons are unaware of KEYTRUDA for this indication.

3. What's Missing

Gap 1: No Post-Launch Quantitative Adoption Data

ATU W1 is pre-launch (March 2025). W2 was expected August 2025. As of April 2026, we are ~10 months post-approval with no quantitative measure of actual KN-689 uptake vs. baseline. The listening sessions and HCP-Pt conversations provide qualitative signal, but the quantitative adoption curve is unmeasured.

Gap 2: No QLEX-Specific Conversation Data Yet

The SubQ IO study kicked off November 2025 with 7 conversations collected. This is too small for pattern detection. The QLEX J-code only became permanent April 1, 2026 — the real adoption signal will emerge Q2-Q3 2026.

Gap 3: No Patient-Reported Outcomes or Experience Data

All studies are HCP-perspective (even HCP-Pt conversations are analyzed through the HCP lens). Patient journey mapping — including time from diagnosis to NAT initiation, experience with PD-L1 testing delays, and satisfaction with KN-689 treatment — is absent.

Gap 4: No Competitor IO Tracking in HNSCC

The studies focus on Keytruda/KN-689 without systematic competitive tracking in HNSCC. The ATU W1 shows 61-63% awareness of the Opdivo regimen — competitive monitoring is needed to understand whether Opdivo awareness converts to prescribing intent.

Gap 5: No Longitudinal Surgeon Behavior Tracking

Multiple studies identify surgeons as the key bottleneck, but no study tracks surgeon behavior over time. A surgeon-specific longitudinal panel — measuring referral patterns, PD-L1 ordering rates, and MDT presentation rates — would close the most important measurement gap.

Gap 6: No Community-Specific Intervention Testing

The community vs. academic split is well-documented, but no study measures the effectiveness of specific community-targeted interventions (peer endorsement, simplified pathway tools, telemedicine MDT access). The ATU W1 finding that community ONCs prescribe more aggressively (32% vs. 15%) deserves deeper investigation.

4. Implications for ZoomRx

4.1 Research Priorities

  1. ATU W2 acceleration: The post-launch quantitative data is overdue. If not already in field, prioritize fielding with expanded surgeon sample and community-heavy panel. Include QLEX adoption questions.

  2. Surgeon-specific module: Design a standalone surgeon engagement study (qual + quant) measuring referral behavior, PD-L1 ordering comfort, MDT presentation rates, and KN-689 experience. This fills the most important gap across all studies.

  3. QLEX adoption tracker: Leverage the SubQ IO study to specifically track QLEX-vs-IV conversations, with a post-J-code pulse check in Q2-Q3 2026. If QLEX adoption accelerates, this becomes a flagship study for Merck's LOE defense team.

  4. Cross-indication perioperative platform: Package NSCLC (KN-671), HNSCC (KN-689), and urothelial (Padcev+Keytruda) referral pathway and MDT dynamics as a cross-indication offering. The patterns are consistent — ZoomRx can offer a single methodology that scales across tumor types.

  5. Community vs. academic deep-dive: The 32% vs. 15% prescribing split needs explanation. A targeted qual study among community oncologists who have adopted KN-689 early would reveal what drives community adoption and how to scale it.

4.2 BD Opportunities

  • KN-689 launch tracker (annual): Recurring ATU + HCP-Pt conversation + listening sessions. The baseline exists; the ongoing tracking is the revenue stream.
  • Surgeon engagement study (new): Standalone study targeting the identified bottleneck. Could be pitched as "Surgeon Ready" or "MDT Pathway" study.
  • QLEX adoption study (new): Merck's LOE defense team needs primary research on QLEX adoption drivers/barriers. The SubQ IO study is a foot in the door.
  • Biomarker testing longitudinal tracker: Reflexive PD-L1 testing adoption is a leading indicator. Quarterly tracking by institution type fills a gap no other firm is measuring.
  • Community early adopter study: Understand and replicate what's driving the 32% community adoption rate.

4.3 Cross-Client Insights

The HNSCC MDT dynamics and surgeon referral patterns are relevant to J&J's RYBREVANT Head & Neck expansion (ORIGAMI-5). If Rybrevant gains approval in R/M HNSCC, many of the same MDT workflow and surgeon engagement challenges will apply. ZoomRx has a head start in understanding this ecosystem via Merck studies.

5. Thesis Check

Thesis Status Evidence from Synthesis
1. QLEX adoption determines post-2028 floor TOO EARLY Only 7 SubQ conversations collected; J-code permanent Apr 1 2026. Need Q2 data.
2. KN-689 slow burn — surgeons are bottleneck STRONGLY SUPPORTED Every study confirms surgeon-controlled referral chain. PD-L1 testing infrastructure: only 4/13 reflexive. 85% vs 65% MDT presentation gap. Community surgeon "not yet convinced."
3. Perioperative TAM expansion is right bet STRENGTHENED NSCLC and HNSCC data both show untapped perioperative populations. ATU shows 50% of HCPs unaware of new treatments.
4. MDT is the intervention point STRONGLY SUPPORTED ATU quantifies: 85% SURG MDT presentation vs 65% ONC perception. Biomarker qual, KN-689 report, and NSCLC study all converge. 20-40% leakage documented.
5. Three-pronged LOE defense TOO EARLY QLEX component unmeasured; patent litigation not in scope; perioperative stickiness untested.
6. Community adoption may outpace academic NEW — EMERGING ATU W1: 32% community vs 15% academic prescribe complete regimen. Counterintuitive but may reflect guideline adherence over individualization. Needs W2 confirmation.

Back to TA Knowledge Hub | Home

4.1 Research Priorities


ATU W2 acceleration: The post-launch quantitative data is overdue. If not already in field, prioritize fielding with expanded surgeon sample and community-heavy panel. Include QLEX adoption questions.


Surgeon-specific module: Design a standalone surgeon engagement study (qual + quant) measuring referral behavior, PD-L1 ordering comfort, MDT presentation rates, and KN-689 experience. This fills the most important gap across all studies.


QLEX adoption tracker: Leverage the SubQ IO study to specifically track QLEX-vs-IV conversations, with a post-J-code pulse check in Q2-Q3 2026. If QLEX adoption accelerates, this becomes a flagship study for Merck's LOE defense team.


Cross-indication perioperative platform: Package NSCLC (KN-671), HNSCC (KN-689), and urothelial (Padcev+Keytruda) referral pathway and MDT dynamics as a cross-indication offering. The patterns are consistent — ZoomRx can offer a single methodology that scales across tumor types.


Community vs. academic deep-dive: The 32% vs. 15% prescribing split needs explanation. A targeted qual study among community oncologists who have adopted KN-689 early would reveal what drives community adoption and how to scale it.


4.2 BD Opportunities

KN-689 launch tracker (annual): Recurring ATU + HCP-Pt conversation + listening sessions. The baseline exists; the ongoing tracking is the revenue stream.
Surgeon engagement study (new): Standalone study targeting the identified bottleneck. Could be pitched as "Surgeon Ready" or "MDT Pathway" study.
QLEX adoption study (new): Merck's LOE defense team needs primary research on QLEX adoption drivers/barriers. The SubQ IO study is a foot in the door.
Biomarker testing longitudinal tracker: Reflexive PD-L1 testing adoption is a leading indicator. Quarterly tracking by institution type fills a gap no other firm is measuring.
Community early adopter study: Understand and replicate what's driving the 32% community adoption rate.

4.3 Cross-Client Insights
The HNSCC MDT dynamics and surgeon referral patterns are relevant to J&J's RYBREVANT Head & Neck expansion (ORIGAMI-5). If Rybrevant gains approval in R/M HNSCC, many of the same MDT workflow and surgeon engagement challenges will apply. ZoomRx has a head start in understanding this ecosystem via Merck studies.
